concepts.title: Galvanometer
concepts.conceptBody:
Galvanometer 

Many common devices including car instrument panels, battery chargers measure potential difference, current or resistance using d' Arsonval Galvanometer. It consists of a provided coil placed in the magnetic field of a permanent magnet. Attached to the coil is a spring. In the equilibrium position, with no current in the coil, the pointer is at zero and spring is relaxed. When there is a current in the coil, the magnetic field exerts a torque on the coil that is proportional to current. As the coil turns, the spring exerts a restoring torque that is proportional to the angular displacement. Thus, the angular deflection of the coil and pointer is directly proportional to the coil current and the device can be calibrated to measure current. The maximum deflection, typically 90 to 120 is called full scale deflection. The essential electrical characteristics of the galvanometer are the current ${{i}_{g}}$ required for full scale deflection (of the order of $10\mu \text{A}$ to $10\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ mA}$ ) and the resistance $\text{G}$ of the coil (of the order of 10 to $1000\text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ }$ ).

The galvanometer deflection is proportional to the current in the coil. If the coil obeys Ohm's law, the current is proportional to potential difference. The corresponding potential difference for full scale deflection is,

$\text{V}={{\text{i}}_{\text{g}}}\text{G}$

concepts.title: Ammeter
concepts.conceptBody:
Ammeter 

It is a device used to measure the current through a circuit element and connected in series with that element.

Now, $I=\left( \frac{\varepsilon }{R} \right),{I}'=\frac{\left( \frac{\varepsilon }{\text{R}} \right)}{1+\left( \frac{\text{r}}{\text{R}} \right)}$

$\therefore $ $\text{{I}'}=\frac{\text{I}}{1+\left( \text{r}/\text{R} \right)}$

Few important points to note: 

(1) The reading of an ammeter is always less than the actual current in the circuit elements.

(2) Smaller the resistance of the ammeter, more accurate is the reading obtained.

(3) An ideal ammeter has zero resistance.

(4) To convert a galvanometer into an ammeter, a small value resistance called shunt is connected in parallel across the galvanometer.

To convert a galvanometer into an ammeter which can measure a current I:

${{I}_{\text{g}}}\equiv $ full scale deflection current

$\text{I}\equiv $ required current range.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \left( I-{{I}_{g}} \right)R=\left( {{I}_{g}}G \right) \\ R=\frac{{{I}_{g}}G}{I-{{I}_{g}}} \\ \end{array}$

where $R$ is the internal resistance.

concepts.title: Voltmeter
concepts.conceptBody:
Voltmeter 

It is a device which can measure the potential difference across a circuit element and connected in parallel with that element. 
 

${{\text{V}}_{\text{observed }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\left( \text{{I}'R} \right)$

$\therefore $ $(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I}) \mathrm{r}=(\mathrm{I} ' \mathrm{R})$

$\text{Ir}=\text{{I}'}\left( \text{r}+\text{R} \right)$

$\text{{I}'}=\frac{\text{Ir}}{\left( \text{r}+\text{R} \right)}=\frac{\text{I}}{\left( 1+\text{R}/\text{r} \right)}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{{V}'}<\text{V} \right)$

As $\text{r}\to \infty ,\left( \text{{I}'}\to \text{I} \right)$

Few important points to note:

(1) The reading of the voltmeter is always less than the actual value.

(2) Greater the resistance of the voltmeter, more accurate is the reading obtained.

(3) For an ideal voltmeter, resistance should be infinite, which has been realized in practice through a potentiometer.

To convert a galvanometer into voltmeter of given range $\text{V}$ : 

We connect a high value resistance $R$ is series with the galvanometer.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{i}}_{\text{g}}}\text{R}+{{\text{R}}_{\text{g}}}{{\text{i}}_{\text{g}}}=\text{V} \\ {} & \text{R}=\frac{\text{V}}{{{\text{i}}_{\text{g}}}}-{{\text{R}}_{\text{g}}} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Potentiometer
concepts.conceptBody:
Potentiometer

The potentiometer is an instrument that can be used to measure the emf or the internal resistance of an unknown source. It also has a number of other useful applications.

Principle of Potentiometer 

The principle of potentiometer is schematically shown in figure. A resistance wire ab of total resistance ${{R}_{ab}}$ is permanently connected to the terminals of a source of known emf ${{E}_{1}}$. A sliding contact $c$ is connected through the galvanometer $G$ to a second source whose emf ${{\text{E}}_{2}}$ is to be measured. As contact $\text{c}$ is moved along the potentiometer wire, the resistance ${{R}_{cb}}$ between points $c$ and $b$ varies. If the resistance wire is uniform ${{\text{R}}_{\text{cb}}}$ is proportional to the length of the wire between $\text{c}$ and $\text{b}$. To determine the value of ${{\text{E}}_{2}}$, contact $\text{c}$ is moved until a position is found at which the galvanometer shows no deflection. This corresponds to zero current passing through ${{\text{E}}_{2}}$. With ${{\text{i}}_{2}}=0$, Kirchhoff's second law gives,

${{\text{E}}_{2}}=\text{i}{{\text{R}}_{\text{cb}}}$

With ${{\text{i}}_{2}}=0$, the current $\text{i}$ produced by the emf ${{\text{E}}_{1}}$ has the same value no matter what the value of emf ${{\text{E}}_{2}}$. A potentiometer has following applications.

To find emf of an unknown battery:

We calibrate the device by replacing ${{\text{E}}_{2}}$ by a source of known emf ${{\text{E}}_{\text{K}}}$ and then by unknown emf ${{\text{E}}_{\text{U}}}$. Let the null points are obtained at lengths ${{l}_{1}}$ and ${{l}_{2}}$. Then,

${{\text{E}}_{\text{K}}}=\text{i}\left( \rho {{\text{l}}_{1}} \right)$

And ${{\text{E}}_{\text{U}}}=\text{i}\left( \rho {{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)$

Here, $\rho =$ resistance of wire ab per unit length.

$\therefore $ $\frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{K}}}}{{{\text{E}}_{\text{U}}}}=\frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}$

Or ${{\text{E}}_{\text{U}}}=\left( \frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{{{\text{l}}_{1}}} \right){{\text{E}}_{\text{K}}}$

So, by measuring the lengths ${{l}_{1}}$ and ${{l}_{2}}$, we can find the emf of an unknown battery.

To find the internal resistance of an unknown battery:

To find the internal resistance of an unknown battery let us derive a formula.

In the circuit shown in figure

$i=\frac{E}{R+r}$ … (i)

and $\text{V}=$ potential difference across the terminals of the battery

$\text{V}=\text{E}-\text{ir}=\text{iR}\ldots \left( \text{ii} \right)$ … (ii)

From equations (i) and (ii), we can prove that

$r=R\left( \frac{E}{V}-1 \right)$

Thus, if a battery of emf $\text{E}$ and internal resistance $\text{r}$ is connected across a resistance $\text{R}$ and the potential difference across its terminals comes out to be $\text{V}$ then the internal resistance of the battery is given by the above formula. Now let us apply it in a potentiometer for finding the internal resistance of the unknown battery.

The circuit shown in Fig. is similar to the previous one.

Hence $\text{E}={{\text{i}}_{1}}\rho {{l}_{1}}$ … (i)

Now a known resistance $\text{R}$ is connected across the terminals of the unknown battery as shown in Fig. below

This time ${{\text{V}}_{\text{cb}}}\ne \text{E},$

But ${{\text{V}}_{\text{cb}}}=\text{V}$

where $\text{V}=$ potential difference across the terminals of the unknown battery. Hence,

$\text{V}=\text{i}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ … (ii)

From Eqs. (i) (ii),

$\frac{\text{E}}{\text{V}}=\frac{{{\text{l}}_{1}}}{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}$

Substituting in $r=R\left( \frac{E}{V}-1 \right)$, we get

$r=R\left( \frac{{{l}_{1}}}{{{l}_{2}}}-1 \right)$

So, by putting $R,{{l}_{1}}$ and ${{l}_{2}}$ we can determine the internal resistance $r$ of unknown battery.
